Type 2(HSV-2) spreads by kissing infected persons and spreading by sexual contact. In rare circumstances, herpes (HSV-1 and HPV-2) can be transmissible through kissing. However, the number of outbreaks/recurrences may reduce over time.

TargetRLF1: The only way to know if you have chlamydia, also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection is to get treatment. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamidium. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina/urogenital swab specimen to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of CHlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use one of two methods to gather your sample: Swab test. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample.
